extra_docs.extra_documentation: When set to swMateAlignALIGNED, vectors normal to the selected entities point in the same direction. When set to swMateAlignANTI_ALIGNED, vectors normal to the selected entities point in opposite directions.

COLORREF values encode RGB components using the formula: color_value = MAX(MIN(red_rgb_value,255),0) + MAX(MIN(green_rgb_value,255),0)*16*16 + MAX(MIN(blue_rgb_value,255),0)*16*16*16*16. This formula clamps each RGB component to the range 0-255 and packs them into a single integer.
A color value of -1 indicates that the color is not defined or is using a default setting.

This method restores the model from a rollback state created by AccessSelections. The typical workflow is: call AccessSelections to put the model into rollback and access the feature's selections, then either call IFeature::ModifyDefinition or IFeature::IModifyDefinition2 if you modified the feature, or call this method if you did not modify the feature. Failing to call either this method or ModifyDefinition leaves the model in an invalid rollback state.

The corresponding AccessSelections method puts the model in a rollback state to allow access to the selections that define the feature. Use ReleaseSelectionAccess to restore the rollback state if you did not modify the feature, or use IFeature::ModifyDefinition or IFeature::IModifyDefinition2 if you did modify the feature.

When setting balloon size, specify -1 to use the document default balloon size. Document default values can be retrieved using IModelDocExtension::GetUserPreferenceInteger with swUserPreferenceIntegerValue_e.swDetailingBOMBalloonFit and swUserPreferenceOption_e.swDetailingNoOptionSpecified, or set using IModelDocExtension::SetUserPreferenceInteger with the same preference keys and a swBalloonFit_e value.

The size must be appropriate for the associated fastener type. Valid sizes can be found in the Element Specification section of the Advanced Hole PropertyManager by selecting a type from the Type dropdown and inspecting the Size dropdown.
The size corresponds to a Size value selected in the Thread PropertyManager for the selected thread Type.
This method is obsolete and has not been superseded. Returns true if the animation is positioned at the end, false if the animation controller is not running.

Removes the progress indicator from the SOLIDWORKS status bar. The removal is not immediate; instead, a show-window event is posted to the message queue, and the progress bar is removed after Windows processes the message. Returns true if the removal request is successful, false otherwise.
This property uses degrees (range 0.0 to 360.0) unlike most other Angle properties in the API which use radians.
The rotation angle must be in the -360 to +360 degree range. Values outside this range wrap around to be mapped back into range. For example, setting 365 degrees results in 5 degrees.
Returns -1 if this property is invalid for the selected entities.

Layers are only supported in SOLIDWORKS drawing documents. The bit indicators for visual properties are: color = 0x1, style = 0x2, width = 0x4. When getting this property, the returned bit value indicates which properties have been overridden from the layer defaults. When setting this property, the input bit value indicates which properties should maintain their current override values; other properties will be reset to the layer's default settings. Passing 0 resets all visual properties to use the default settings of the item's layer.

By default, items created on a layer inherit the visual properties defined by that layer. However, specific visual properties (color, style, width) can be overridden on individual items. When an item is not on any layer, this property returns an undefined value. Use the Layer property to determine the layer name; if an empty string is returned, the item is not on a layer and this property is not used.

This property should only be set when you want to reset specific visual properties to the defaults of the owning layer. To change or set specific values for visual properties, use the dedicated property setters such as Color, Style, and Width rather than this property.

Not all item types support all visual property overrides. For example, sketch hatches and sketch points currently support only the color visual property (0x1 bit value), while annotations and sketch segments support color, style, and width overrides.

Instead of specifying this property, you can use IModelDocExtension::SelectByID2 to pre-select the entities to mate. You can pre-select mate entities during mate creation, but not during mate editing.
Populate the array of this property by using either IModelDocExtension::SelectByID2 or IModelDocExtension::SelectByRay and ISelectionMgr::GetSelectedObject6.

Layers are supported only in SOLIDWORKS drawing documents. The return value might be an empty string because an old document might not contain layers or if the entity has been generated in a new document that does not have layers defined.
An empty string indicates no layer. If you set the layer to a layer name that does not exist, nothing happens and the layer remains unchanged.
Unlike other Layer properties that return a string, this property returns an integer index of the layer. Returns -1 if the layer is not defined.
Returns -1 if the property is invalid for the selected entity type.

Accepts either a numeric value or an equation that starts with an equal sign (=). If DiameterOverride is false, the default value is derived from the diameter of the circular edge specified by the Edge property.
This property is only valid when the corresponding DiameterOverride property is set to true.
The meaning of the diameter value depends on the DiameterType property: it can represent the major diameter, minor diameter, or conical offset from the edge.
This is a get-only property; the set accessor is not implemented. The property is only valid when AreaStyle is set to swPMIDatumTargetAreaStyle_X or swPMIDatumTargetAreaStyle_Circular.
This property does not support swTapered and swTaperedDrilled hole types.

Common end condition values used across multiple feature types include: swEndCondBlind (extends a specified distance), swEndCondThroughAll (extends through all geometry), swEndCondUpToNext (extends to the next surface), swEndCondOffsetFromSurface (extends to a surface with an offset), and swEndCondUpToSelection (extends to a selected reference). The specific enum type and valid values depend on the feature type.

For features with end condition references, companion methods GetEndConditionReference and SetEndConditionReference are typically available to access or modify the geometry entity that defines where the feature terminates. See Accessing Selections that Define Features for additional details on working with these references.
End conditions may work in conjunction with other properties such as Distance, OffsetDistance, and direction-reversal flags to fully define the extent of the feature.
Some end condition properties are read-only after feature creation. In such cases, modification requires calling a dedicated method to update the feature parameters as a whole.
If this property is set to true, use the corresponding Diameter property to specify the override diameter.

You can specify either a numeric value or an equation that starts with an equal sign (=). The default value is 0.01 m. When getting the value, it includes any offset that has been applied to the blind end condition reference.
The leader attachment side setting is a characteristic of the annotation itself, not of individual leaders. Therefore, you can get or set it regardless of whether leaders are currently displayed.
If leader display is disabled for the annotation, then the number of leaders returned is 0.

Use IModelDoc2::DeSelectByID instead of this method. This method does not work well when a PropertyManager page is open or a command is running. IModelDoc2::DeSelectByID handles selection correctly whether or not a command is running.

To select or deselect sketch-based objects, the owning document needs to be open and visible. Selections are accessible through the ISelectionMgr of the owning document, even if the owning document is not active. Selection or deselection does not work for sketch objects in a component within an assembly or in a document within a drawing. Selection or deselection of sketch objects owned by the top-level assembly or drawing works, but only if that document is active. If the owning sketch was active (or inactive) when the object was obtained, then it must be active (or inactive) to deselect it.
Index is 0-based.
The returned object is of type DisplayData (implementing IDisplayData) and should be cast from System.object to access its properties and methods.

For line width properties: In drawing documents, objects can be created on a layer that has specific visual properties. By default, the object takes on the visual properties defined by the layer. The line width value specified with this property allows you to override the default layer width. Use the LayerOverride property to determine if the object is using its default layer line width.
This property must be set before the control is displayed. The value is in dialog units relative to the group box that the control is in. The width of the group box is 100.
If FixedAspectRatio is true, then the image is uniformly scaled when either the width or height is changed. To have the width automatically stretch to fit a selected entity, use FitWidth.
This property is valid only if AreaStyle is set to swPMIDatumTargetAreaStyle_Rectangular. This is a get-only property; set is not implemented.
When restoring the rollback state after calling AccessSelections, you can use either IFeature::ModifyDefinition or IFeature::IModifyDefinition2 if you modified the feature.
IFeature::IModifyDefinition2 can also be used as an alternative to IFeature::ModifyDefinition for restoring the rollback state if you modified the feature.

The TopDoc parameter should be a ModelDoc2 object, and the Component parameter should be a Component2 object (or null when modifying a feature in a part), even though the signature may show System.object types.

The TopDoc parameter should be a ModelDoc2 object, and the Component parameter should be a Component2 object or null when modifying a feature in a part. When modifying a feature in a part, pass the IModelDoc2 for the part as TopDoc and set Component to NULL. When modifying a feature in an assembly, pass the IModelDoc2 for the assembly as TopDoc and pass the IComponent2 object in which the feature is to be modified as Component.

This method puts the model into a rollback state to allow access to the selections that define the feature. You must restore the rollback state by calling either IFeature::ModifyDefinition or IFeature::IModifyDefinition2 if you modified the feature, or the corresponding ReleaseSelectionAccess method if you did not modify the feature. Failure to restore the rollback state will leave the model in an unstable state.
This method is cyclical; it loops back on itself and does not return null. This behavior differs from other GetNext methods which typically return null or nothing at the end of a sequence.
Returns null or nothing when the last item is reached, indicating the end of the iteration sequence.

Returns Nothing or null when no more items exist. This method is typically used with a corresponding GetFirst method to traverse through a collection. When this method returns Nothing or null, you have reached the end of the list.
The sheet must be visible for this method to work. See ISheet::SheetFormatVisible.

To traverse items in a view, call the corresponding GetFirst method to get the first item, then call GetNext repeatedly (using the count from a GetCount method if available) to access the remaining items.
To traverse all documents, ISldWorks::GetFirstDocument must have returned the first IModelDoc2 object that calls this method.
The returned object is of type CThread, though it may be returned as System.object and require casting.

You can use this method to determine the entity type and the appropriate function calls allowed for the specific type. COM implementations can also use QueryInterface to determine the underlying interface supported by this object.
Call this method before calling GetArrowHeadAtIndex or IGetArrowHeadAtIndex to determine the valid range of indices for accessing individual arrow heads.

Mark values are used by functions that require ordered selection. If an object is already selected with a mark and you attempt to select the same object again with Append = true and a different mark value, the object remains selected with its original mark value. Reselecting a previously selected object does not assign a new mark value.
The mark value, which was previously passed as an integer parameter in the superseded Select method, is now controlled through the Data parameter's SelectData object.
When UseDoc is true, the method uses the document default text format setting and ignores the TextFormat parameter, which should be set to null.
The TextFormat parameter should be an ITextFormat object, not a generic object.
To see the effects of changing the text format information, use ModelDoc2::GraphicsRedraw2.

For notes containing embedded rich text information, using this method to set a specific text format has no effect and returns false. To set a specific text format for such notes, first set the note to use the document default text format, then set the specific text format.
To set the text format, you must be editing the sketch. Use IModelDoc2::EditSketch or ISketchManager::InsertSketch to edit the sketch before calling this method.
Value must be between 0 and 1, inclusive.
Represents the intensity of a light source illuminating a surface from all directions without attenuation or shadowing.
Distance values are returned in model units (e.g., meters). A typical value might be 0.0022098 m. The specific units depend on the document's unit system settings.
The name cannot contain any of the SOLIDWORKS reserved special characters.
The name cannot contain any of the SOLIDWORKS reserved special characters.

This is an in-process method that is not supported in VBA, VB.NET, C#, and C++/CLI. In unmanaged C++, it works with a pointer to an array of Component2 objects. You must call the corresponding count method first to determine the size of the array before calling this method.
Call the corresponding GetComponentCount method before calling this method to determine the size of the array that will be returned.

To change the text format, get the ITextFormat object using this method, use its APIs to set the desired text format properties, then use SetTextFormat to apply the changes. To make the object use the default document text format instead of a local format, use SetTextFormat with the UseDoc argument set to True.

The color value is calculated as: specular_color = MAX(MIN(red_rgb_value,255),0) + MAX(MIN(green_rgb_value,255),0)*256 + MAX(MIN(blue_rgb_value,255),0)*65536. This formula converts individual RGB component values (0-255) into a single integer representation. See SOLIDWORKS Help for more information about appearances.
Returns false if no out-of-date SpeedPak configurations exist that require updating.
This property is valid only when the corresponding Size property is set to swBalloonFit_e.swBF_UserDef. Available since SOLIDWORKS 2012 FCS, Revision Number 20.0.
The return value is an IFeature object, even though the method signature may show System.object for type compatibility. In .NET, cast the result to Feature or IFeature as appropriate.
Available in SOLIDWORKS 2004 FCS, Revision Number 12 and later.
This method can be called multiple times to add multiple explode views to the configuration.
Perfect explode views often require user interaction. This method attempts to create an explode view automatically based on the current conditions.

To change the text format of an entity, retrieve the ITextFormat object using the appropriate GetTextFormat or IGetTextFormat method, modify the text format properties as needed, then apply them using SetTextFormat or ISetTextFormat. To revert to using the document default text format, call SetTextFormat or ISetTextFormat with the UseDoc argument set to True.
For examples of link strings usable with swBalloonTextContent_e.swBalloonTextCustomProperties, see INote::PropertyLinkedText.

Components may be unloaded when the assembly document is opened with the 'Do not load hidden components' option enabled, in addition to Quick View or Selective open modes. All returned arrays are parallel and correspond by index to the same unloaded component.
The visual effect depends on the position of the light source and the position of the viewer. Increasing the value makes the material appear shinier.
For examples of link strings usable with swBalloonTextContent_e.swBalloonTextCustomProperties, see INote::PropertyLinkedText.
For BOM balloons specifically, you can only get or set the lower text using INote::GetBomBalloonText or INote::SetBomBalloonText after inserting a BOM balloon.
Set the returned definition object to Nothing or null when it is no longer needed. Verify that the returned object is valid before use, as not all types may be supported.
When you programmatically change the visibility of a graphical element, you may need to redraw the graphics window using IModelDoc2::GraphicsRedraw2 to see those changes reflected on screen.

To get or set document default balloon style values: use IModelDocExtension::GetUserPreferenceInteger with swUserPreferenceIntegerValue_e.swDetailingBOMBalloonStyle and swUserPreferenceOption_e.swDetailingNoOptionSpecified for getting; use IModelDocExtension::SetUserPreferenceInteger with the same preferences and a swBalloonStyle_e value for setting. Specifying -1 uses the document default balloon style.

In drawing documents, annotations can be created on a layer that has specific visual properties. By default, the annotation takes on the visual properties defined by the layer. The style value specified with this property allows you to override the default layer style. Use the LayerOverride property to determine whether the annotation is currently using its default layer line style. Annotation style is currently supported only in SOLIDWORKS drawings.

Style is a combination of Boolean values, each represented by a bit in this long value. The different Boolean values are represented in the corresponding enumeration. The control style must be set before the PropertyManager page is displayed, except when setting values that explicitly allow runtime modification (such as swPropMgrPageComboBoxStyle_EditBoxReadOnly, which can be set either before or after the PropertyManager page is displayed).

This property value is a combination of several boolean values, each represented by a bit in this long value. You can only set this property before the PropertyManager is displayed. By default, text in PropertyManagerPageLabel is left-justified.

If you do not set any display properties, then the value is set to 0, which means: slider is horizontal, slider has no ticks, and your application is only notified when the user is done dragging the slider to move it.
This property might automatically load the model in the view. To get or set the shape intensity of a jagged cut break line, use the ShapeIntensity property.

By default, only one list item can be selected at a time. When another list item is selected, that item becomes the active item and the previously selected item is cleared. To enable multi-selection, use swPropMgrPageListBoxStyle_MultipleItemSelect. Methods supporting multi-selection include GetSelectedItems, IGetSelectedItems, GetSelectedItemsCount, and SetSelectedItem.

You must set this property before the PropertyManager page is displayed. For a selection list to automatically have a horizontal scroll bar if the text is wider than the selection box, set this property's style to swPropMgrPageSelectionBoxStyle_HScroll.

Control points determine the shape of a spline or curve. Starting with a set of control points, all other points of a spline can be calculated. A given point on the spline is calculated using the polynomial basis functions of its nearest control points. The knot vector determines which control point basis functions are in force for calculating each point along a curve.
The control point coordinates in the returned array are double-precision floating-point values.
Use this method to determine the size of the array before calling the corresponding component retrieval method (e.g., IGet* methods).

When deleting many items sequentially, use IFeatureManager::EnableFeatureTree to temporarily disable FeatureManager design tree rebuilding before performing deletions, then re-enable it afterward to improve performance.
This property is read-only. The relief depth is set during initialization and cannot be changed afterward.
The default value is 0.0003683 m (approximately 0.0145 inches).
When UseReliefRatio is false, SOLIDWORKS uses ReliefDepth with ReliefWidth to define the relief geometry. When UseReliefRatio is true, ReliefRatio is used instead.
For cylindrical or conical swept flanges, this property is valid only during feature creation. For regular swept flanges, it remains valid after creation.
If this property is True, SOLIDWORKS uses the ReliefRatio property. If it is False, SOLIDWORKS uses the ReliefDepth and ReliefWidth properties.
This property is valid when UseDefaultBendRelief is false and ReliefType is swSheetMetalReliefRectangular or swSheetMetalReliefObround.

The returned CustomBendAllowance object includes information about the type of bend allowance as defined in swBendAllowanceTypes_e, a numeric bend allowance value, a K-Factor value, and may reference a bend table file path.
This method is valid only if UseDefaultBendAllowance is false. You can change the custom bend allowance after it has been initialized by accessing the parent sheet metal.

This method replaces the deprecated BendAllowance, BendAllowanceType, BendTableFile, and KFactor properties. The bend allowance value (double), bend allowance type (int), bend table file path (string), and K-factor value (double) are now accessible through the CustomBendAllowance object returned by this method.
Bend radius is specified in meters.
Returns true if the selections are successfully accessed, false if not.
For cross-language compatibility, when modifying a feature in a part, the Component argument can be specified as Nothing in VB.NET or null in C#.
Default value is 0.0003683 m.
This property is valid when ReliefType is swSheetMetalReliefRectangular or swSheetMetalReliefObround.
SOLIDWORKS uses this property with ReliefDepth only if UseReliefRatio is false. If UseReliefRatio is true, then ReliefRatio is used instead.

The default bend relief refers to the bend relief settings from the original sheet metal feature. When not using the default (when this property is false), the relief configuration depends on UseReliefRatio: if UseReliefRatio is true, then ReliefRatio is used; if UseReliefRatio is false, then ReliefDepth and ReliefWidth are used instead.
For cylindrical or conical swept flanges, this property is valid only during feature creation.

The returned array includes all table annotations for the table feature, including any splits. Because many ITableAnnotation APIs apply to all parts of a split table annotation, you may only need to work with one annotation from the returned array. For example, changing the grid line thickness on one table annotation affects all splits of that table annotation. Call GetTableAnnotationCount to get the total number of table annotations, including any splits.

This method can be used to obtain the array of table annotations all at once instead of calling GetFirstTableAnnotation and then repeatedly calling ITableAnnotation::GetNext to obtain the table annotations.
Relief ratio is calculated as d / part thickness, where d is the width of the relief cut and the depth by which it extends past the bend region. Valid range is 0.05 to 2.0.
When UseReliefRatio is false, SOLIDWORKS uses ReliefDepth and ReliefWidth properties instead of the relief ratio.
Call this method before calling IBendTable::IGetTableAnnotations to get the size of the array for that method.
Normally there is one table annotation per table feature. Split tables have separate table annotations, increasing the count.
Normally there is one table annotation per table feature. Split tables have separate table annotations, increasing the count.
Normally there is one table annotation per table feature. Split tables have separate table annotations, increasing the count.

Call this method before calling ITitleBlockTableFeature::IGetTableAnnotations to get the size of the array for that method. Normally there is one table annotation per table feature. Split tables have separate table annotations, increasing the count.
This count includes all table annotations in the view, including split table annotations where applicable.
Normally there is one table annotation per table feature. Split tables have separate table annotations, increasing the count.

See Accessing Selections that Define Features for additional details on using this property.
Returns 0 if there are no items.

This property is valid only when creating the feature in a non-sheet-metal part.
When true, the thickness and bend radius properties can get and set user-defined values. When false, those properties are invalid as setters and only return default values.

When true, indicates that the thickness value specified in the gauge table is overridden, allowing use of a custom thickness value instead.
This property takes precedence over UseGaugeTable. If set to true, UseGaugeTable is automatically set to false and cannot be changed.
The ICustomBendAllowance object can specify a bend allowance type defined by the swBendAllowanceTypes_e enumeration, a bend table file path, a K-Factor value, or a numeric bend allowance value.
This method is valid only if the UseDefaultBendAllowance property is set to false.

The CustomBendAllowance parameter encapsulates bend allowance settings that were previously set via separate properties: BendAllowance (double value), BendAllowanceType (integer), BendTableFile (string path), and KFactor (double value).
When set to true, geometric parameters such as bend radius and thickness are determined by values in the gauge table rather than being manually specified.
Excel gauge tables are used to specify geometric parameters. When true, parameters such as bend radius and thickness are controlled by the gauge table values rather than manual specification.
You can use the ISketch interface for extracting information about geometry in the sketch.
The return value is specifically a Sketch object, though typed as System.object in this method signature and must be cast appropriately.

Each drawing view contains an underlying sketch. The user can activate the sketch for a drawing view by double-clicking the view. Once the drawing view is active, you can add sketch geometry directly to the view's sketch.
Use this method to obtain the array of notes all at once instead of iterating through notes individually, which provides a more efficient way to access all notes.
The return value is a Surface object. The SurfaceIn parameter should be a Surface object.
The Point parameter is a reference to a double array representing the point coordinates.
When setting the name, it should not contain any special characters reserved by SOLIDWORKS.
When changing the name, ensure it is unique and does not contain the at-sign character (@). Before changing the name, call IFeaturemanager::IsNameUsed to verify the name is unique and valid.
After setting the name, you must rebuild the FeatureManager design tree using IFeatureManager::UpdateFeatureTree to make the change visible.

When setting the name, it must be unique in the FeatureManager design tree. If the name is not unique, the name change will fail. Changes to the name are not visible to the user until a rebuild has been performed.
Length is specified in meters.
Returns -1 if the property is invalid for the selected entity.
After changing the length using this property, call IDragArrowManipulator::Update to display the modified handle in the graphics area.
Valid values range from 2 to 100. If an invalid value is specified, SOLIDWORKS does not change the balloon stack.
This method can be used with trimming curve creation routines (for example, ISurface::AddTrimmingLoop2) to construct a trimmed surface.
Any existing object created by this method is destroyed if you call this method again.
The IAnnotation object is a higher-level object that contains methods and properties that apply to all types of annotations.

This method returns null or nothing for center marks created as features (earlier style of center marks). If ISelectionMgr::GetSelectedObjectType3 equals swSelCENTERMARKS, then GetAnnotation equals null or nothing. If ISelectionMgr::GetSelectedObjectType3 equals swSelCENTERMARKSYMS, then GetAnnotation points to the IAnnotation object.

Return value: True if the attribute's value is set, false if not. Attributes are string notes that have tag names and are not read-only. Use GetAttributes or IGetAttributes to get attributes for a block instance, and GetAttributeValue to retrieve an attribute's value.

The profile curve must be of type line, circle, or B-spline curve. The method creates an infinitely long tabulated cylinder by extruding the profile curve along the specified axis direction vector. The ProfileCurve parameter is an ICurve object, and the return value is an ISurface object. Any existing object created by this method is destroyed if you call this method again.
When this property is false, use the GetCustomBendAllowance method to retrieve the custom bend allowance value.
Changing the value of this property does not affect geometry until IFeature::ModifyDefinition or IFeature::IModifyDefinition2 is called.
The return value is an array of doubles describing the x, y, and z values of the base point. The array contains 3 doubles representing the coordinates.

Scale value must be greater than 0. Only applies to non-associative distance dimensions (e.g., those created using IDrawingDoc.CreateLinearDim4 or IDrawingDoc.CreateDiamDim4). For other dimension types (associative dimensions or angular measurements), setting the scale has no effect and getting the scale returns 1. After setting this property, use IModelDoc2.GraphicsRedraw2 to redraw the graphics window to see the changes.
Valid range is 1% to 1000%. This value is only used when IPageSetup.ScaleToFit is set to false.
Valid range is 0.0000001 to 500000. The getter of this property is obsolete and has been superseded by ISketchBlockInstance.GetScale3.

To zoom in by a factor, get the current scale using this property, multiply the return value by the desired factor, then set the result back using this property. When using data from this property along with IModelView.Orientation3 and IModelView.Translation3, multiply in this order: orientation, then scale, then transform. To map coordinates from 3D space to the screen, use IModelView.Transform.
This property only applies to SOLIDWORKS standard textures.
This property supersedes IFaceHatch.Scale, which had a get-only implementation with a non-functional setter.
Valid input curves are b-curves.

The Curve1In and Curve2In parameters should be Curve objects, and the return value is a Surface object. The Apex parameter should be passed by reference as ref System.double when working with the array of 3 doubles in C#/.NET.

The count does not include subfeatures. Subfeatures include mate features within a mategroup, drawing views on a sheet, and similar nested features. A subfeature can be identified by whether it can be returned by GetFirstSubFeature or GetNextSubFeature methods.

Call this method before calling IGetFeatures to determine the size of the array to pass into that method. When counting all features (including child features), the method does not count features in components.
Returns NULL when no texture is present on the entity in the specified configuration.
Available since SOLIDWORKS 2005 FCS, Revision Number 13.

The features returned by this method can be in any order. Do not rely on the order to indicate anything about parent-child relationships or hierarchy. If hierarchy and order information is needed, use methods like FirstFeature, GetNextFeature, GetFirstSubFeature, and GetNextSubFeature to retrieve the information.
Before calling this method, call GetFeatureCount to determine the size of the array that will be returned.
The array contains exactly 9 doubles in the order: R, G, B, Ambient, Diffuse, Specular, Shininess, Transparency, Emission.

The ID parameter is calculated as: (Size of the array returned by AddPropertyExtension) - (Position of the property extension in the array). AddPropertyExtension adds property extensions to a last-in-first-out, 1-based array and returns the size of that array.
SOLIDWORKS recommends using the IAttribute, IAttributeDef, and IParameter interfaces instead of this method. These interfaces provide more flexibility.
Call the corresponding GetEdgeCount or GetEdgesCount method before calling this method to determine the size of the returned array.
This is an in-process method that returns a pointer to an array of edges. It is not supported in VBA, VB.NET, C#, and C++/CLI.
This method should be called before calling the corresponding IGetVertices method to determine the required array size for vertex retrieval.

This method removes material property values (such as color) but does not remove the material itself. To remove material from a part, call IPartDoc::SetMaterialPropertyName2 with an empty string for the Name parameter. Return value: True if material properties are successfully removed, false if not.
Call this method before calling the corresponding IGetEdges method to determine the size of the array.

Attribute instances are features and can be suppressed. Always call IAttribute::GetEntityState to check if the attribute instance is valid, unambiguous, and not suppressed (swIsEntitySuppressed). Attribute instances can be retrieved using standard feature traversal functions (IPartDoc::FeatureByName, IModelDoc2::FirstFeature, IFeature::GetNextFeature). Use Feature::GetSpecificFeature2 to get the underlying Attribute object from a Feature object. If an attribute was placed on a document, you must use Feature::GetSpecificFeature2 to retrieve it.
The fixed face is the face that remains in place (stationary) during the feature operation.
See Accessing Selections that Define Features for additional details on using this method.
Sends IGES-specific data to the geometric modeler. The SystemName parameter is defined by the swIGESPreferredSystem_e enumeration. Available since SOLIDWORKS 2001 FCS, Revision Number 9.0.
Call the corresponding GetFaceCount or GetFacesCount method before calling this method to determine the size of the returned array.
See Accessing Selections that Define Features for additional details on using this method.
Available from SOLIDWORKS 2001Plus FCS, Revision Number 10.0
Examples are available demonstrating usage of this method in the context of adding and deleting appearances from specific display states (available in VBA, VB.NET, and C#).
Highlights persist through rotation, zoom, and other repaint actions until explicitly hidden.
To change the highlight color, hide the element first then display with the new color.
The return value is a Face2 object, not a generic System.object as the signature suggests.

The return value and the FromFace1Disp and FromFace2Disp out parameters are Face2 objects, not generic System.object references. The Thickness parameter is returned in meters. Use GetNextFace to iterate through subsequent neutral faces in this midsurface feature.

The array is 0-based. If you skip the third and fifth items, then the array looks like ArrayOut[0]=3 and ArrayOut[1]=5. For patterns that support bi-directional and uni-directional configurations, calculate the pattern instance number to skip as follows: For a bi-directional pattern: I = n2 * (i - 1) + (j - 1). For a uni-directional pattern: I = i - 1. Where I = pattern instance number, n2 = number of instances in Direction 2, i = index for Direction 1, and j = index for Direction 2. In the pattern's PropertyManager, find n2 in Direction 2 > Spacing and Instances > Number of instances field and find [i,j] in the Modified Instances section.

For fill patterns, the array is linear and zero-based and contains the (row_id, column_id) of each pattern instance to skip in the format: [row_id_instance1, column_id_instance1, row_id_instance2, column_id_instance2, ...].

The returned array contains values of type long (integer) representing the edge IDs.

Each edge has a unique edgeId, which is an arbitrary identifier value. If an edge ID value is 0, it indicates that the edge does not lie on a face boundary. A flag value in the array indicates whether the data represents a triangle strip (value = 1) or a single triangle (value = 0). For triangle strips with n vertices, there are 2(n-2)+1 edge tags in the returned data.

For triangle strips, the i-th element of the edge tags array (starting at i=1) represents the tag of the edge between vertex floor((i-1)/2)+1 and vertex floor(i/2)+2, where floor(x) is the integer portion of x. For single triangles, the i-th element (starting at i=1) represents the tag of the edge between vertex i-1 (or vertex n if i=1) and vertex i.

To calculate the Instance parameter: For a bi-directional pattern use I = n2 * (i - 1) + (j - 1), where I is the pattern instance number, n2 is the number of instances in Direction 2, i is the index for Direction 1, and j is the index for Direction 2. For a uni-directional pattern use I = i - 1. In the pattern's PropertyManager, n2 is found in Direction 2 > Spacing and Instances > Number of instances field, and [i,j] values are found in the Modified Instances section. The method returns true if the pattern instance is modified successfully, false if not.

This is an in-process method only. It is not supported in VBA, VB.NET, C#, and C++/CLI. In unmanaged C++, it returns a pointer to an array of trim tools. You must call GetTrimToolsCount before calling this method to determine the correct value for the Count parameter. See In-process Methods for details about this type of method and Accessing Selections that Define Features for additional context.
Some of the returned sketch relation objects may be NULL. Check for NULL before accessing them. You should deallocate any dynamically allocated memory.
You can call GetRelationsCount to determine the number of sketch relations that will be returned.
You must specify the corner treatment type before changing this property from false to true.

The array size is (NumPts * 8), where NumPts is the number of user points. Each point contributes 8 values: Color (COLORREF integer, may be 0 or -1 for default), Line Font/Style (see swLineStyles_e), Line Width (see swLineWeights_e), Layer ID (use ILayerMgr::GetLayerById to obtain the ILayer object), Layer Override (bit flags: color=0x1, style=0x2, width=0x4; e.g., a value of 3 indicates color and style are overridden), and ptLoc[3] (x, y, z coordinates as doubles). Use GetUserPointsCount to determine the number of points.

Trim tools can include planar and non-planar faces, reference planes, reference surfaces, and sketches. This method is only supported in in-process, unmanaged C++; it is not supported in VBA, VB.NET, C#, or C++/CLI.

Weld symbol names follow the format <standard-type>, where standard and type are separated by a hyphen and enclosed in angle brackets. For example, a JIS seam is <jweld-jsm> and an ISO seam is <weld-jsm>. The angle brackets and hyphen are required.
The currently supported list of ISO weld symbols includes: BUTT, BUSQ, BUSV, BUSB, BUSVBR, BUSBR, BUSU, BUSJ, BACK, FILL, PLUG, SPOT, SEAM, SEAMC, JSPT, and JSM.

Calling this method does not change the current working directory to that of the opened file, whereas interactively using the File Open dialog box does. This may affect documents with references. Use ISldWorks::SetCurrentWorkingDirectory to set the working directory before calling this method to mimic File Open dialog behavior.

When opening files that contain references, the current Search Folder settings may affect which references get loaded. If Search Folders are in use, SOLIDWORKS looks for references in the Search Folders before trying the current working directory. Use ISldWorks::GetSearchFolders and ISldWorks::SetSearchFolders to manage these settings.
If this method successfully opens an assembly, it still returns swFileNotFoundError in the Errors parameter if a referenced component file cannot be located.

This method does not activate and display the document if the file is already open in memory in an assembly or drawing. However, it returns a valid IModelDoc2 pointer usable with functions that do not require a document to be displayed. Use ISldWorks::ActivateDoc2 or ISldWorks::IActivateDoc3 to activate and display the document. Because the file is not activated nor displayed, ISldWorks::ActiveDoc or ISldWorks::IActiveDoc2 will not return a pointer to this document.

This method fires the FileOpenNotify2 event. Additionally, the ActiveDocChangeNotify and ActiveModelDocChangeNotify events are sent if the file being loaded is not already open as the active document.

To avoid large increases in memory usage when adding parts to assemblies: Opening a model causes SceneGraph to display the model. SceneGraph uses maps with default sizes of 2MB-3MB for even the simplest model, and assemblies and parts do not share the same SceneGraph buffer. To avoid memory increases: (1) Set the document to invisible, (2) Open the parts, (3) Set the document to visible, (4) Add the part to the assembly. See ISldWorks::DocumentVisible for details.
This method honors the swLargeAsmModeEnabled user preference toggle. If this toggle is set to true, then the file opens in lightweight mode.

These four GetBoundingBoxDirection methods together define the slicing volume boundaries. They must be called after pre-selecting the first slicing plane, and their behavior and return values depend on whether linear or radial slicing mode is active. Direction 1 returns values in the range 0.0 to 500.0, which provides a reference for the expected scale of the bounding box manipulator values.
The terms 'angular slicing' and 'radial slicing' refer to the same slicing mode, as distinguished from linear slicing.

For COM applications, the returned object pointer can be used to call any APIs on the ISketchSegment interface. The underlying ISketchArc object can be obtained using QueryInterface on the returned ISketchSegment object. OLE applications can define a new ISketchSegment or ISketchArc object using the returned Dispatch pointer. Visual Basic applications interpret the pointer automatically, allowing direct calls to ISketchSegment or ISketchArc functions.
